TTRPG-Stuff/Agenda-For-Next-Campaign-Dis.filePath: TTRPG Stuff/Agenda For Next Campaign Discussion.md
TTRPG-Stuff/Agenda-For-Next-Campaign-Dis.title: Agenda For Next Campaign Discussion
TTRPG-Stuff/Agenda-For-Next-Campaign-Dis.content:
Quick run through of the rules of Chime.


How do you understand Narrative Action vs. Tactical Action? What do you enjoy about one and the other, and how we can put these in concretely into the rules system of our next campaign?


What Aspects do we need to account for when running Theatre of the Mind Combat?

Zone based combat?
We definitely need to get better at placing ourselves in Scenes, i.e. add descriptions of where our characters are when performing an action. We won’t have a map like in Owlbear Rodeo to do this for us.
Distances measured in Arm, Room, Field, Horizon.
Takes one move action to get from Room to Arm.
Takes 2-3 move actions to get from Field to



What do you prefer about Scene vs. Procedure based play?


Discuss difference between Procedural vs. Procedurally Generated (systems for how play works rather than random generation).


Based on responses, for the next campaign there’ll be a couple dungeon like environments that might have a map, but mostly the procedures will consist of navigation of the island (Days divided into 6 Watches), with scene-based play within the different parts of the island.




Sandbox Play

Can Players Make Clear Goals and Sub Goals Themselves, i.e. keeping goals clear through organization, before the game.
Very limited play-space (5 mile radius island) with many factions to ally / backstab / contend with, so shouldn’t feel aimless if people are conscious about making aim at something.



Book-keeping:

What Inventory System? e.g. 10 items or only one Bulky, or Troika 2d6?



Lethality

A Necessary Evil to add impact.
Adding the Time / Gear / Skill system to reduce die rolls / risk if playing cleverly.
Can Add Legacy Systems to keep a Narrative Going.



Summary of Preferences
Diego

Does Not Want Technical Character Builds.
Prefers Set Track Character Advancement, As Well As Some Diagetic Advancement.
Prefers Single World Builder.
Prefers a Grounded World With Some Random Nonsense Thrown In (e.g Harry Potter).
Prefers Scene Play to Randomly Generated Play.
Prefers Heroic Play, but not Invincible.
Wants a Less Lethal Game than DCC, But Still Lethal.
Wants Clear Goals, But Still Non-Linear Ways to Achieve Those Goals.
Worried that Procedural Play Makes Clear Goals Lose Focus.
Wants More Recurring Characters in Play.
The Book-Keeping Tasks I Enjoy Are Keeping Track of Rumors & Mapmaking.
Favorite Adventure was Frozen In Time. Like Encounters Connected to the Story.
Enjoyed the Navigation Elements of the Dark Tower.
Felt Lost in Mega-dungeon. Would Prefer Smaller Goals I Can See Direct Progress Towards.

Katie

Prefers A Simple Character Who Is Developed As The Game Progresses.
Benchmarks to Advance in Level in Terms of Character Development?
Prefers Gonzo Storytelling.
Prefers Riskiness Tieing Into Story, But Not Risk That Doesn’t Tie Into Story.
Problems with Money in the Game.
Lethal but Not Cruel.
Prefers Less Technical Combat.
Mind Blanks Out if in Open World with no goals to follow.
Prefers Investigative Play.

Liam

Prefers Simple Character Creation
Interested in Diagetic Advancement.
Wants Tactical Action (Decisions in Combat Should Be Interesting Rather Than Just Die-Rolls Back and Forth)
Prefers Subsistance Characters.
Does Not Want To Book Keep.
Wants Lethal Gameplay.
Likes puzzles and creative problem solving. Combat shouldn’t be the only way to achieve objectives and decision-making in combat should be significant and not simply a dice roll slugfest.
Likes choosing my own goals, choosing faction alignment.
Likes having a massive environment, and many different ways to traverse that environment.

Patrick

I want much less combat. Wants to make decisions that determine who our characters are.
I want to play characters rather than just roles.
I want characters with explicitly defined goals, and potentially explicitly defined weaknesses. The goals must be compatible with the goals of other players, and it has to be a goal relevant to the world we’re playing in.
I want players to more explicitly think through their character’s motivations.
I want to be empowered to create a fun story rather than trying to optimize around the achievement of goals.

Hayley

Likes Character Building, Character Arcs, and Storytelling.
Lethal Games do not allow  enough time to get attached to characters.
Scene Play leads to better storytelling for obvious reasons.
Fears Sandbox Play leads to aimless wandering

Alec

Does not want to play a campaign based purely around surviving life.
Does not want to do much homework (e.g. developing backstories / worldbuilding outside of the game).

Gary

Likes complex character creation
Prefers XP based character systems.
More Grounded Setting.
More Procedure vs. Scene Play.
More Handwavey vs. Bookkeeping.

TTRPG-Stuff/Character-Creation-Design-No.title: Character Creation Design Notes
TTRPG-Stuff/Character-Creation-Design-No.content:
Let’s start by discussing the Character Creation Rules in Chime. Here’s the main points:
Character Attributes
Character Attributes are intentionally paired down from the 5-6 stats of D&D and related games to just three stats:

STRength is a general stat related to overall body condition.
DEXterity represents overall physical coordination.
WILLpower represents overall mental condition.

I wanted to deemphasize the idea that your characters are defined by your Attributes, and instead emphasize that characters are determined by player choice. Your character is up to you: there’s no Intelligence stat telling you whether to play clever or foolish, no Charisma stat determining whether you should be likable or abrasive.
The reduction to three stats also means that each stat is more impactful in play (i.e. in DCC, for most characters Constitution was probably used far more often than Personality, whereas with three stats you can be fairly sure that all three attributes will come up often).
Failed Careers
Every Character has a Failed Career, which serves as inspiration for who your character is - but doesn’t necessarily have to (after all, this is a Career that you have failed at), to compensate for the fact it’s randomly assigned. It also gives you some funky items to get started with, rather than having to waiting to get fun items.
Character Development
One of Patrick’s main hope for the next campaign was to push players to develop their character’s beliefs and motivations more deeply than in our DCC campaign. At the same time, several players also preferred starting with a loosely defined character and discovering them gradually through play. To support both approaches, I came up with some scaffolding for character creation. At the end of each session, each player answers one of several prompts to help them reflect on what kind of character they’re playing. Players who developed their character further at the beginning should already have an answer for these questions, where as this should help the more loosely defined characters become defined over play.
I also wanted to build some scaffolding that encourage players to think about their connections to other members of the party, so at the end of each session we’ll also discuss the current party’s connections and how these may change over time. During the Dungeon Crawling of the last couple months of DCC the procedures of the game didn’t push for much character interaction, so I’ve also chosen to add a procedure that pushes Characters to converse whenever they stop to recover their HP.

TTRPG-Stuff/Metacurrencies-in-Chime.title: Metacurrencies in Chime
TTRPG-Stuff/Metacurrencies-in-Chime.content:
I have two hesitations about using Meta-currencies like Inspiration and Stunting because of my game-design goals in this Campaign, but maybe we can work out something similar that motivates players?
My first hesitation is that one of my primary goals with this campaign is to encourage players to engage with the world from within, i.e. in a Fiction-First way (as described in the Player Expectations section of the rules). Because of that, I want as many mechanics a possible to be Associated Mechanics, in the sense of this post by the Alexandrian: thealexandrian.net/wordpress/17231/roleplaying-games/dissociated-mechanics-a-brief-primer
Meta-currencies subtly shift how players think about the game. Rather than “What would my Character do here within the world?” the question becomes “When is the optimal time to spend this meta-currency to influence the narrative in a way I as a Player prefer?“. I don’t think that’s inherently bad design - a lot of games use these systems very effectively - but with the particular design goals I had in mind for this particular campaign I don’t think it would work well within the game.
The second issue is more of a me problem. I’m very bad at keeping up with ad-hoc systems as a GM - I much prefer to use more structured procedural systems to help me carry a game forward, while I use most of my mental capacity to help interpret the world for the players and to respond to their actions. We experimented with ad-hoc reward systems in our DCC campaign back in the day, with a system called Fleeting Luck, to reward characters doing something cool, but I was never able to remember to award it regularly enough.
That said, players will drift to playstyles that reward them, so I wonder if there’s a way to capture some of that spirit without interrupting the Fiction First playstyle. One possibility might be that at the end of each session, after play has concluded, we as players nominate a player whose character had a particularly memorable or interesting roleplaying moment, and reward them in some way for that. Right now the game doesn’t use XP, so the reward structure would need some thought, but I think that kind of post-session recognition could work well. It’s something I can add to my list of things to do after I finish a game, so I don’t forget to do it, and it also avoids interrupting the Fiction First play-style because it occurs at the end of a session, after everyone’s finished playing their characters.

TTRPG-Stuff/Playing-in-a-10+-Player-TTRP.filePath: TTRPG Stuff/Playing in a 10+ Player TTRPG Game.md
TTRPG-Stuff/Playing-in-a-10+-Player-TTRP.title: Playing in a 10+ Player TTRPG Game
TTRPG-Stuff/Playing-in-a-10+-Player-TTRP.content:
This is likely your first TTRPG game with more than 10 players. Such a game brings it’s own challenges, but also it’s unique opportunities. It’s not something we get to do very often, so let’s enjoy it for what it is!
To smooth over the rough parts of large player games, here’s some extra expectations to plan for that we wouldn’t normally keep as strictly in a game with fewer players.
Simultaneous Movement
The Dark Tower is a dangerous environment, and as such we won’t be using exploration turns at all during play, only Rounds. So your movement speed will be capped to your movement speed during rounds rather than during exploration turns (on the order of 30’ +/- penalties rather than 90’, moving up to double that if not performing an action). We’ll take all characters movement simultaneously, at the top of the turn order. You’ll have 10 seconds to move your character, I’ll narrate what you see aas you move, and then we’ll begin any relevant actions the characters will perform.
Turn Length
Optimally, each person’s turn during a game with this many players should take 5-10 seconds each. In practice it’s not going to happen every time. For instance, you may want to take an action you’re not sure how to Pre-Roll (see below), or you might slip up and not finish Pre-Rolling by the time it gets to your turn. But we need to set the expectation that turns will be kept this short more often than not, to ensure the game keeps moving, and players are not stuck waiting for their turn for large amounts of time.
Please review relevant rules / spell descriptions ahead of the game, so you can make informed decisions quickly during the game.
You’re also welcome to take your character’s turn to ask questions about your surroundings. But to ensure things keep moving, understand that this’ll likely be the only thing you get to do during your turn (e.g. your character is taking the turn to examine the environment rather than take an action).
If it’s not your turn, you can interrupt the turn order by calling a TIME OUT to roleplay, discuss plans collectively, etc. I’m not going to put any limitations on how many TIME OUTS can be called, or how long they can be, but understand that it’s all our collective responsibility to ensure the game keeps moving, so keep this in mind when calling TIME OUTS. I’ve also installed a chat client in Owlbear Rodeo so that if it’s not your turn you still have a way of communicating with other players without calling a TIME OUT or interrupting the progression of turns.
Planning in Advance, and Pre-Rolling
Always keep track of when your characters will get a turn, and plan what action(s) you will take well in advance. By the time there are ~3 characters ahead of you, you should have already committed to an action, and if necessary and possible, begun to pre-roll your action. If you are not committed to an action by the time your character’s turn comes up, I will have to skip your turn to keep the game moving (e.g. your character is spending the turn thinking of what to do). You can also spend a turn examining your environment (i.e. asking questions to the GM) rather than taking an action.
Pre-Roll whenever possible. If your action requires a die roll you already know how to do, do all the relevant rolling before your character’s turn comes up. When your turn arrives, you should more often than not be able to clearly narrate your action, and the outcome of the action.
In cases where characters repeatedly fail to Pre-Roll in time, I may choose to delay the results of a declared action until the following turn of the character.
Consider the following examples:
Spellcasting
Ticemius decides to cast sleep well before the GM gets to him in the turn order. Before the GM gets to him, Ticemius has already rolled to cast the spell (a 21!), looked up the effect (4 targets fall asleep for d6 hours), and rolled the d6 (they’ll fall asleep for 4 hours). When the GM gets to Ticemius, he can concisely narrate his actions:

“Ticemius palms a handful of glowing sand from a pouch and hurls it at the 4 frontmost snake-men, who need to make a DC21 Will Save or they’ll fall asleep for 4 hours.”

Attacking
Logart commits to attacking the frontmost skeletal amalgamation in front of him well before the GM gets to him. He thinks of a mighty deed (Decapitation), looks up the AC (16) of the amalgamation on Owlbear, and makes the attack (rolling a 14), deed die roll (3), and damage roll (11, plus the 3 from the deed die). The roll is a success, so by the time the GM gets to Logart, he can clearly narrate his actions:

“My Warhammer smashes one of the Skeletons Skulls into dust, with a Deed Die roll of 3, and it takes 14 Damage.”

Note that the only numbers that the GM needs from the player are the Deed Die roll (the higher the deed die the better the interpreted effect), and the overall damage, so the other numbers don’t need to be narrated publicly to the other players. If Logart misses, if he doesn’t feel like narrating much he could just say

“I attacked and missed”

or if he feels like embellishing,

“Logart slips upon the bone-dust covering the corridor, and misses his mark”.

Sneaking
Robbi Hobbington decides to travel ahead of the party, trying to hide in the shadowed corridors. He’s note sure if the GM will call a sneak roll, but if his action may require a sneak roll, it might be worth making the roll ahead of time, so that if the GM asks for the result, Robbi will already have it on hand.
Other Ideas
If you have other ideas about how to speed up play, let me know and we can clarify these before the game starts tomorrow.

TTRPG-Stuff/Shared-Party-Motivations.title: Shared Party Motivations
TTRPG-Stuff/Shared-Party-Motivations.content: Right now all Characters have a pre-defined entry into the Campaign, which connects them in some way. They’re all Characters from a single dominant city in the region they come from, which is just called The City in the rules right now (the Failed Careers Section probably fleshes out The City, though in a minimalist way). They’re all Characters who tried to have a normal career, and failed at it. They’re all Characters who have been imprisoned for some reason, whether rightfully or wrongfully. And they have all been brought together because they’ve accepted a bargain with the Martel Company, and in return been assigned a Collective Debt via a Curse, which they must pay off periodically or waste away. Do you think some stronger motivation to stay together is needed? If so, I think that’s something you should pose as a question in the larger group chat, i.e. what overall characters would you like to play together?
